responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Babies in the Consulting Room: Discussion

Jennifer Stuart and Kehinde Ayeni

Kehinde Ayeni

Kehinde Ayeni  

In each of the three cases presented in this section, we can see the interplay of two forces. On one hand, there is what Daphne de Marneffe (2004) has called “maternal desire”—a wish to have and care for children, which may vary in intensity and expression from one woman to the next. For some, maternal desire has the kind of urgency we might associate with a drive, and its thwarting—for example, by infertility—may be a psychological catastrophe; Jane Kite (2007) has written beautifully about this elsewhere. On the other hand, there are feelings of uncertainty and ambivalence about mothering, and toward children. In every mother, there are fears of what Barbara Almond (2010) has called “the monster within”—an assortment of feelings that may range from ordinary resentment in a “good enough” mother to more pernicious excesses of hostility. In some women, fears of that monster may be muted; in others, they are quite pronounced. Proscriptions against the expression of maternal ambivalence also may vary in strength from one patient—and one analyst—to the next.

In analytic treatment surrounding a baby's presence in the consulting room, we might expect to see strands of maternal desire and maternal ambivalence—arising from both patient and analyst—intertwine. The analyst, then, must work to help a patient develop her better mothering angels, while allowing the “monster within” to have its say. One possible strategy is for the analyst to take refuge in her own, best mothering self, noticing and fostering all that is good in the patient's relationship with her child.

Jennifer Stuart, faculty, The Institute for Psychoanalytic Education affiliated with NYU Medical School. Kehinde A. Ayeni, faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality and Psychoanalysis

Sweet Dreams: Erotic Plots. By Robert J. Stoller. London: Karnac Books, 2009, xviii + 249 pp., $36.95 paperback.

Melvin Lansky

Melvin Lansky

10921 Wilshire Road, Suite 509
Los Angeles, CA 90024-3909
E-mail: mrlansky@att.net

Melvin Lansky  

Sweet Dreams: Erotic Plots was published almost twenty years after Robert Stoller's unexpected and tragic death in 1990. The book's existence came to light when Sybil Stoller handed Professor Richard Green a manuscript by her late husband that had languished on a bookshelf in her home for sixteen years, having been returned when his publisher discontinued its series of psychoanalytic books.

Stoller notes at the outset that the book is unique, even among his original contributions, in that it considers pornography not simply as a sexually arousing picture or text, but as a conscious daydream, a naked primal daydream that not only summarizes the individual's erotic preferences but mirrors his or her character in ways that go far beyond the erotic component. Stoller's transformative definition of pornography requires us to think in new terms about the transformation he has outlined from the sexually arousing object to the daydream. In this regard, it is important to note that the dearth of psychoanalytic attention accorded the daydream allows the unquestioned assumption that daydreams (Freud 1908; Person 1995; Goldberger 2008) can be dealt with solely in terms of manifest content and do not require the detailed analysis we know is required with night dreams.

As a psychoanalytic candidate at what was then the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute, I had the privilege of being in one of the few classes that Stoller taught to candidates. Because of the demands made on his time by his prolific writing, his editorial work for psychiatric and psychoanalytic journals, his university duties, and his presentations on the national and international level, he seldom accepted invitations to teach at the institute. He was an electrifying teacher, one who drew students—UCLA medical students, whom he taught regularly, residents, and psychoanalytic candidates—into the topic or text that he was teaching. The course I attended, like the book at hand, possessed an immediacy not unlike taking a walk with Stoller down Melrose Avenue, the porno district of Los Angeles.

Aaron Esman  

Jean-Martin Charcot (1825-1893) is one of the immortal figures in the history of medicine. For students of the central nervous system he is honored as the father of neurology, the first to identify and define such illnesses as multiple sclerosis, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and tabes dorsalis. For psychiatrists he rescued hysteria from the misogynistic mythology under which it had labored for centuries and addressed it as a disease to be studied scientifically.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Is There a Nonverbal Period of Development?

Ellen Rees

Ellen Rees

Ellen Rees  

Psychoanalysts have from the earliest days of our discipline been working to understand and articulate the place of language in mental life and therapeutic change. This is a more complex question than it might seem. Words emerged from gestures and sounds relatively late in our evolutionary history, and in individual development spoken words emerge only after a time. How then do words fit into and function in a bodily context in which communication predates words? Freud (1915) described a relationship between words and their origins in bodily experience using the concepts of word presentation and thing presentation. He was well aware that these aspects of speech and language are somehow joined but that each functions differently in the mind: “But word-presentations, for their part too, are derived from sense perceptions, in the same way as thing-presentations are” (p. 202). Freud hypothesized that perceptual experiences are represented unconsciously as fragments of sensory experience (visual, acoustic, tactile, etc.) that are associated with objects, things, in the physical world. Thus, these thing presentations are composites of memory traces from various sensory domains that are then associated with words for things. But words are themselves rooted in memories of perceptual experiences, of a sound that was heard (a “sound image”), a word that was seen (a “visual image”), and memories of movements of the muscles concerned with speech and of the hands (a “kinaesthetic image”) (Freud 1891). Psychoanalysts might well wonder how these modes, nonverbal and verbal, influence a mind. Scholars and researchers, too, have wondered and have contributed to the effort to explore how these two kinds of signifiers function, together and separately, in shaping subjective experience.

More Meaning: Commentary on Vivona

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

24 East 82nd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel Stern  

This article by Jeanine Vivona is the latest in a series of her papers (Vivona 2003, 2006, 2009) addressing language in psychoanalysis. A book on the subject is also in preparation (Vivona forthcoming). In this body of work, which ranges expertly and thoughtfully across both empirical research findings and clinical psychoanalysis, Vivona has taken the perspective that language is given too little attention and granted too little significance in contemporary psychoanalysis. Her readers get the impression that she has strong feelings on the subject, although we must read between the lines to reach that conclusion, because Vivona always thinks and writes carefully and precisely, and avoids stridency, preferring to allow the accumulation of evidence to speak for her.

Another hallmark of Vivona's work is the inexorability of her arguments. She marshals evidence so masterfully that one is hard pressed to imagine coming to a conclusion different from hers. Beginning to read this essay is like stepping into the top of a chute at a water park: there is only one route to the end, and the trip takes place without your having to make a lot of decisions along the way. Vivona begins by demonstrating that infants are capable of perceiving and making use of the sounds of adult speech, goes on to challenge the deeply entrenched notion that language, because it can symbolize only selectively, violates the “full texture of lived experience,” and then pulls the argument together by demonstrating that infants understand and use some of the meanings of aural speech. She concludes that there are grounds to challenge the long-standing belief, inside psychoanalysis and outside it, that there exists an exclusively nonverbal and sensorimotor period of infancy (see also Litowitz 2011). The conceptual importance of that challenge is that, if there is no such period,

Deborah Browning

Deborah Browning

29 East 9th Street
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: deborah.browning@nyu.edu

Deborah Browning   Adjunct Associate Professor, Department of Psychology, NYU Graduate School of Arts and Sciences.

Biography is by no means a homogeneous genre—neither through the course of history nor as it is written today. Its relationship to psychoanalysis, that other kind of life-story project, is controversial, while its place within historical writing has often been minimized and viewed as second-class (Caine 2010). Questions raised concern the nature of the data to be used to (re)construct the person's life and how far one may go inferentially with the material at hand. Just how personal should the biography become in order to convey the subject's character and personality? And does it need to? And how important is it to embed the individual's life and development within a contemporary social, cultural, and political context? (Kohut 1986, 2003).

If one approaches this study of Ernst Freud—third son and fourth child of Sigmund and Martha Freud, husband of Lucy Brasch, father of Stephan, Lucian, and Clemens, and a successful Berlin society architect—with curiosity into his inner world, his conflicts, fears, and fantasies, and his way of experiencing and relating to the significant people in his world, then one can only be disappointed. There is little offered here in that regard. This was clearly not the intent of the author, Volker Welter, a German-born professor of the History of Art and Architecture at UC, Santa Barbara, who was educated first in Berlin and then at the University of Edinburgh.

But if one wants a fascinating, impeccably researched, and carefully documented image of less-well-known aspects of the early history of psychoanalysis, including the development and design of the two public psychoanalytic clinics in Berlin, all accompanied by plentiful pictures and descriptions of the Freud-designed private homes and consulting rooms of several prominent first-generation analysts in Berlin and London, then this book should satisfy well. And there is more to recommend it, at least to a psychoanalytic audience interested in the psychology of architecture and the psychoanalytically charged social atmosphere of bourgeois modernism that existed in Weimar Germany.

The Function of Suffering as Portrayed in the Scarlet Letter and Reflected in Clinical Work

Diane Donnelly

Diane Donnelly

3626 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: ddonnelly30@gmail.com

Diane Donnelly   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

Suffering is commonly seen as an unconscious effort to alleviate painful feelings of guilt. However, suffering also aims at averting loss of ego functions and hence loss of mental stability. This second function of suffering is discussed in the light of Freud's observations of characters wrecked by success and Weiss's ideas about mutual love as a threat to mental stability. Hawthorne's portrayal of Arthur Dimmesdale in The Scarlet Letter (1850), biographical material about the author, material from his diaries, and material from a psychotherapy case and an analysis illustrate the function of suffering to preserve mental stability in the face of heightened success and happiness. Hawthorne, it is argued, intuitively grasped this function of suffering in his novel.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toward an Ethics of Desire for Psychoanalysis: Introduction to Kirshner

Ellen Rees

Ellen Rees

Ellen Rees  

Lewis Kirshner invites psychoanalysts to look again at the question, Is there an ethics intrinsic to psychoanalysis? Using Lacan's seminar The Ethics of Psychoanalysis as his foundation, he hypothesizes that “psychoanalysis as a discipline and as a set of institutions embodies intrinsic ethical problems …” (p. 1224). These problems are rooted in desire: the desire of the analyst, of analytic groups, and of society. Kirshner urges us to make the exploration of the impact of these desires more central in psychoanalytic training and to consider ways to provide for review of psychoanalytic practice.

The ethical considerations that interest Kirshner form three strands in the discussion. The first involves the assumptions, the values, and the goals of psychoanalysis itself. What is the nature of human beings? What constitutes a good and healthy life? The second concerns the analyst's desire and the impact of the analyst's fantasies. What led the analyst to want to be an analyst? What does the analyst want from psychoanalysis and from the analysand? The third is the influence of psychoanalytic groups, institutions, and practices and the society and culture in which they exist and function. How are these influences expressed in ideologies, forms of power, and economic interests? Can safeguards for ethical standards be institutionalized?

The complexity and intensity of the psychoanalytic process contribute to a paradoxical situation for the analyst concerning the “good” of the analysand. How do psychoanalysts preserve an analysand's individuality and autonomy while thinking about treatment goals? How do they think about and work with wanting something from or for the analysand? Kirshner and his commentators, Lawrence Friedman, Jonathan Lear, and Mitchell Wilson, lead us through the shifting perspectives that inform these questions and shape the landscape of an ethical domain.

Félix Velasco-Alva begins his book on an integrative approach to treating couples by noting that the theory and practice of couples therapy tends to fall into two broad models, one based on systems theory and the other on psychoanalysis. According to Velasco-Alva, because this field comprises biological, social, intrapsychic, and interactional phenomena, an integrative model is needed. Thus, his interest in systems theory has led him to find in constructivisim a point of contact with psychoanalysis. In this original contribution, Velasco-Alva proposes a new model for working with couples, which he calls “The Psychodynamic-Constructivist Model, with an Intersubjective Focus.”

A word on the title is warranted: this book is anything but a simple treatment manual. Readers will find here Velasco-Alva's rich integration of advances in intersubjectivist and constructivist approaches in psychoanalysis and related therapies.

Sybil Houlding

Sybil Houlding

129 Church Street, Suite 308

Sybil Houlding  

Psychoanalysis did not become a socially significant phenomenon in Italy until the 1969 IPA Congress in Rome. In an introduction to Stefano Bolognini's paper on empathy (a version of which appears as chapter 8 of the book under review1), Marco Conti (2011) helpfully contextualized his colleague's work by telling us that in Italy, “lacking important pioneers such as Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, Donald Winnicott, or Heinz Hartmann, it took many years for us to get a good idea of what psychoanalysis and its many points of view were all about.” Italian analysts spent many years studying and absorbing the various schools, and meeting with and being supervised by foreign colleagues. Thus their work is inevitably pluralistic. Bolognini has studied North and South American perspectives, as well as British and French authors, and has integrated this reading into his own unique voice.

The book is divided into three parts. The first, “Three Prefaces to the Discussion,” contains three short essays that introduce readers to the author's way of thinking. In chapter 1, “Freud's ‘Objects,’” he writes, “The discussion … I will develop is not in favor of a generic theoretical-clinical eclecticism, but … it is in favor of the recognition of plurality and complexity of our contemporary utilizability in interchanges among colleagues and in the privacy of daily theoretical-clinical reflections” (p. 10).

1[Editors' note] “When this clinically rich, thought-provoking book is translated into English, it will undoubtedly reach the wider readership it deserves.” So wrote Delia Battin (2010) in a JAPA review of this book's Italian original. To this end, we offer a second review of the book, now available in English.621

Peter Heller's A Child Analysis with Anna Freud: The Significance of the Case for the History of Child Psychoanalysis

Nick Midgley

Nick Midgley

21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3 5SD
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: nick.midgley@annafreud.org

Nick Midgley   Director, M.Sc. Programme in Developmental Psychology and Clinical Practice, Anna Freud Centre / University College London.

A Child Analysis with Anna Freud, a collection of Anna Freud's detailed case notes of her treatment of the young Peter Heller between 1929 and 1932, was first published in English in 1990. Not only does this work give us direct access to Anna Freud's ways of thinking and working at a crucial period in the early history of child analysis; it is also one of the few records of an adult reflecting in depth on the experience of being in analysis as a child. Yet to date this work has received little attention in the psychoanalytic literature. In an attempt to redress this neglect, the Heller case study is placed in the context of Anna Freud's emerging ideas about child analysis. In particular, its significance in the development of her psychoanalytic thinking is investigated in the light of her 1927 book, The Technique of Child Analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Is There a Nonverbal Period of Development?

Jeanine Vivona

Jeanine Vivona

11 Bala Avenue
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004
E-mail: jvivona@tcnj.edu

Jeanine Vivona  

That the infant lives in a nonverbal sensorimotor world is widely believed. Yet this notion is being challenged by research, in a range of fields, that depicts the young infant as actively attuned to aural speech from birth and able to process and use its sounds and meanings well before the end of the first year. Following a selective review of the research on infant speech processing between birth and age twelve months, three questions are identified that highlight the theoretical and clinical implications of this research for psychoanalytic conceptualizations of infancy, the nature of language, and clinical process. The research, it is concluded, identifies the operation of linguistic and conceptual processes in early life, processes that may intersect with the experiential and emotional processes with which psychoanalysis is already concerned. Moreover, this research raises questions about the view that the nature of infancy is essentially nonverbal.!

A Prospective Study of Psychoanalytic Practice and Professional Development: Early Career Interviews

Sabrina Cherry , Juliette Meyer , Luke Hadge , Madeleine Terry and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

285 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: sc26@columbia.edu

Steven Roose   Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University; Director, Neuro-Psychiatry Research Clinics, New York State Psychiatric Institute; faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

In 2003 the Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research began a prospective study of graduates designed to both describe and understand their professional trajectory. The study has two components: a quantitative component based on an anonymous comprehensive questionnaire given analysts yearly starting with their graduation, and a qualitative component comprising analyst interviews beginning at the end of the first postgraduate year and repeated every two years. Analysis of the first six years of the qualitative study shows that analysts will talk openly about their practice and careers and that when they do, practical issues are a dominant concern. Analysts both immersed and not immersed in four-times-weekly analytic cases experiment with adapting skills developed in training to treat cases in analysis seen less frequently. Analysts without four-times-weekly case immersion are engaged in analytic careers, participate as faculty at the institute, and report a high degree of career satisfaction. The major findings of this study compel changes in psychoanalytic training programs. The field would do well to address actual clinical practice experience in institute curricula and training programs, thus making analytic training more relevant.

Emerging Views on Gender and Sexuality: Celebrating Twenty Years of New Perspectives on Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, and Trans People

Kim Gelé , Susan McNamara , Sidney Phillips , R. Shelby , Gary Grossman , Susan Vaughan and Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

11 South Main Street, Suite 2
Middletown, CT 06457
E-mail: sxmcnam@mac.com

Ralph Roughton   Former Director and Training and Supervising Analyst, Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute; founding Chair, APsaA Committee on Issues of Homosexuality; Clinical Professor Emeritus, Emory University Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences.

In honor of the eightieth birthday of Ralph Roughton, founding chair of the Committee on Issues of Homosexuality, and the twentieth anniversary of that committee, now the Committee on Gender and Sexuality, this essay shares the reflections of four psychoanalysts who have moved the understanding of psychoanalysis and lesbian, gay, bisexual, and trans people into the twenty-first century. In coordinating the contributors, Ethan Grumbach called to mind Roughton's plenary presentation at the 2001 American Psychoanalytic Association annual meeting, in which he discussed the evolving relationship between psychoanalysis and homosexuality. Roughton (2002)

This essay is based on a discussion group co-chaired by Ethan Grumbach and Patrick J. Haggard held at the meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 2012.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Narcissism

Judith Chused

Judith Chused

1805 Randolph Street, NW
Washington, DC 20011-5339
E-mail: jchused@chused.com

Judith Chused   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences and Clinical Professor of Pediatrics, George Washington University School of Medicine and Children's Medical Center, Washington, DC; Emeritus Training and Supervising Analyst, Washinton Center for Psychoanalysis.

Narcissism is a peculiar quality. We all have it, yet it is considered by psychoanalysts to be problematic. In fact, there was a time when narcissistic individuals were thought to be unanalyzable (Ivimey 1951; Rangell 1982), unable to establish a workable transference. Still, I know that when I write or speak, I want my words and thoughts to be well thought of, and that this wish to have others think well of me reflects my narcissistic vulnerability. I think it is impossible to desire without being vulnerable to that desire. However, this subject, the intertwining of desire and narcissistic vulnerability, specifically, the analyst's desires and vulnerabilities, has been little addressed.

We all want to be admired, by colleagues as well as by patients. We know there is “healthy narcissism,” yet the word narcissistic has a negative connotation, used, like borderline, to describe difficult patients, sometimes as a way to protect our narcissism. Not a good use of diagnostic labels, but understandable, for though it is inevitable that in an analytic process that is deeply experienced our narcissistic vulnerability will be challenged, we are not yet comfortable talking about our wounds.

When I speak of the analyst's vulnerability—that is, his vulnerability to narcissistic injury or grandiose certainty—I am not referring to the problems with self-esteem regulation that can lead an individual in any profession to misuse his position to elevate his sense of self at the expense of someone else's autonomy, creativity, or comfort. I suspect that all of us have interacted with teachers, colleagues, or patients who have behaved in such a manner, leaving others feeling diminished by their exercise of power. However, rather than focusing on pathology, I am speaking to the vulnerability present in every analyst.

Heinz Kohut and the self psychologists emphasized the importance of attending to patients' narcissism, and we have learned from their work.

Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 13, 2012. Submitted for publication February 16, 2012.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toward an Ethics of Psychoanalysis: A Critical Reading of Lacan's Ethics

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner   Clinical Professor in Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute.

Lacan's seminar The Ethics of Psychoanalysis (1959-1960) pursues, from a Freudian perspective, a fundamental philosophical question classically addressed by Aristotle's Nichomachean Ethics: How is human life best lived and fulfilled? Is there is an ethic of this type intrinsic to psychoanalysis? Lacan placed the problem of desire at the center of his Ethics. His notorious self-authorized freedom from convention and probable crossing of limits (see Roudinesco 1993) may have led mainstream analysts to ignore his admonition: “At every moment we need to know what our effective relationship is to the desire to do good, to the desire to cure” (Lacan 1959-1960, p. 219). This means that the analyst's desire, as well as the patient's, is always in play in his attempt to sustain an ethical position. An examination of Lacan's seminar highlights this link, but also points to a number of unresolved issues. The patient's desire is a complex matter, readily entangled in neurotic compromise, defense, and transference, and the analyst's commitment to it is also problematic because of the inevitable co-presence of his own desire. Lacan suggested that more emphasis be placed in training on the desire of the analyst, but beyond that a proposal is advanced for the institutionalization of a “third” as reviewer and interlocutor in routine analytic practice. Analysis may not be a discipline that can be limited to a dyadic treatment relationship.

[[RunningHead]]

Is There Language Disconnected from Sensory/Bodily Experience in Speech or Thought? Commentary on Vivona

Wilma Bucci

Wilma Bucci

116 West Neck Road
Huntington, NY 11743
E-mail: wbucci@adelphi.edu

Wilma Bucci  

Vivona offers a picture of “a young infant who not only understands something of the meaning of the speech she hears, but who also uses that speech to organize both her perception and her conceptualization of the world, perhaps beginning as early as three months of age …” (p. 255). She also makes strong claims concerning the clinical implications of this view:

What are the implications of the idea that language shapes the mind from the very earliest days of life? To state this boldly and even provocatively, there may be no form of self-experience that is profoundly or unequivocally beyond the reach of language, at least not in normal development. If this is so, then infancy provides no inherent justification for therapeutic approaches specifically designed to access modes of thought and being that are conceptualized as beyond language because they are before language in some sense [p. 256].

I'll comment here on several of these points: first, evidence concerning the role of language in the first year of life; second, whether there are forms of experience that are beyond the reach of language—and here I will talk not only about infancy but about the entire course of life; and third, the implications of research concerning language development for clinical work.

Language in the First Year of Life

Professor Emerita, Derner Institute of Advanced Psychological Studies, Adelphi University; Director of Research, Pacella Parent Child Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

Active Perception and the Search for Sensory Symbiosis

Peter Goldberg

Peter Goldberg

909 Ordway Avenue
Albany, CA 94706-2115
E-mail: petergoldberg@restio.org

Peter Goldberg   San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

I would like to look at the question of the senses in psychoanalysis not so much from the standpoint of how the analyst listens and takes in data (which is generally taken for granted as the natural role of sensory perception in the analytic situation) as from the standpoint of the active uses of sensory perception. Attention to the domain of sensory perception, especially in working with patients who suffer from what might be described as disorders of the sensorium, reveals the importance of the analyst and patient's engagement at the sensate level and the significance of active perception in the analyst's technique.

Attention to sensory perception in the analytic situation illuminates certain phenomena that otherwise remain obscure and that invite conceptual elaboration. Of particular interest is the way in which immediate sensory experience seems to lend itself naturally to a shared consciousness of the world and how this affects a kind of functional symbiosis between analyst and patient at the sensate level.

This has implications for our understanding of clinical theory and technique. I will propose that the analyst's sensory participation serves a quite specific function, namely, to initiate or catalyze a shared framework of sensate experience in the analytic situation. This I will refer to as the analyst's inductive function, inasmuch as it entails an active dimension of technique that constantly, if unconsciously, seeks to establish a shared sensory state.

The author thanks Adam Blum for help in researching the concepts discussed in this paper. Submitted for publication March 19, 2012.

[[RunningHead]]

Does it Matter if There is a Nonverbal Period of Development? On the Infant's Understanding the Social World and its Implications for Psychoanalytic Therapy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: p.fonagy@ucl.ac.uk

Peter Fonagy  

In her carefully crafted paper, Jeanine Vivona brings together an accumulating body of evidence that points to the infant's sensitivity and attunement to verbal communications.

The Case for a Verbally Competent Preverbal Infant

Vivona introduces us to compelling evidence that even a very young infant's biology prioritizes speech sounds, that they are sensitive to the perceptual patterns associated with different types of words accumulating phonetic, grammatical, and semantic categories over the course of the first twelve months of life, and that, not surprisingly, this occurs in the context of rapidly improving abilities to discriminate between vocalizations within parental and nonparental object relationships. It may be particularly important to psychoanalysts that sensitivity to prosodic features and emotional tone is established very early, probably in the first half of the first year of life (Spence and Moore 2003). In the course of the second half of that year, infants begin to recognize words regardless of the tone in which they are uttered (Singh 2008). Verbal labels emitted by parents clearly modulate the way infants attend to the world outside of them in the first year of life.

Freud Memorial Professor and Head of Research, Department of Clinical, Educational and Health Psychology, University College London; Chief Executive, the Anna Freud Centre.

Introduction: Babies in the Consulting Room: What Happens When Analyst, Mother, and Child Meet?

Jennifer Stuart

Jennifer Stuart

11 Riverside Drive, Apt. 8FE
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: jstuartphd@gmail.com

Jennifer Stuart  

Analytic work with a pregnant woman may foster the psychological gestation of the self-as-mother. For the mother—as for the infant—the developmental process continues well beyond delivery. It should not surprise us, then, that many mothers continue to bring their babies to sessions after birth. Analytic work in this context can affect a patient's sense of herself, and perhaps her competence, as a mother. Yet the presence of babies in the consulting room is an underacknowledged feature of contemporary practice. For this section of JAPA, we have collected three papers that explore some of the unique challenges and opportunities that arise when a patient brings her baby to treatment. Earlier versions of each paper were first presented at a panel, “Babies in the Consulting Room,” at the June 2011 meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in San Francisco.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Operational Analysis of the Clinical Goals of Psychoanalytic Technique

Mark Levey

Mark Levey

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: marklevey@aol.com

Mark Levey  

An operational description is presented of the clinical goals of analytic technique shared by various analytic orientations. This operational schema of their similarities is then used to organize their differences. In this schema, analytic schools differ from one another in three areas: the kind of unconscious content looked for in uncovering; aspects of the process considered subject to direct intervention; and the most effective stance and interventions with which to pursue their shared analytic goals. Organizing the differences between approaches in the context of shared analytic process goals makes it possible to clarify implicit clinical assumptions and to develop empirical rather than theoretical clinical discussions between analytic orientations. Understanding the intent or purpose of an intervention is useful in comparing approaches, exploring clinical controversies, clarifying implicit clinical assumptions, and understanding approaches more deeply in their own terms.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Erotic Transference: Dream or Delusion?

Franco de Masi

Franco de Masi

via Ramazzini 7
Milano 20129
ITALY
E-mail: franco.demasi@fastwebnet.it

Franco de Masi   Training Analyst, Italian Psychoanalytical Society; former President, Centro Milanese di Psicoanalisi; Secretary, Training Institute of Milan.

The erotic transference can be seen as the Janus face of clinical work in psychoanalysis: it may either arise out of the positive emotions necessary for the building of new shared realities, or be fueled by falsified and distorted constructions. In the former case, the erotic transference expresses the capacity to anticipate, or “dream,” the emotional relationship with the object—which is why Freud valued its transformative aspect as one of the “forces impelling [the patient] to… make changes”—whereas in the latter it is equivalent to a flight from psychic reality and may be imperceptibly transformed into an actual delusion.

[[RunningHead]]

Teaching Psychodynamics in the Twenty-First Century

Deborah Cabaniss

Deborah Cabaniss

903 Park Avenue, Suite 2E
New York, NY 10075-0361
E-mail: dsc3@columbia.edu

Deborah Cabaniss  

A lack of clarity in psychoanalytic education has negatively affected the educational experience of candidates now in training and makes it difficult for prospective candidates to understand the field. Three basic educational concepts—learning objectives, operationalizing, and adult learning theory—can help psychoanalysts teach and assess their students in a clearer, more objective way. Using these concepts has broad implications for both psychodynamic and psychoanalytic education in the twenty-first century.

responseSet.title: A Holist's Anxiety of Influence: Commentary on Kirshner
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Holist's Anxiety of Influence: Commentary on Kirshner

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129-B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021

Lawrence Friedman  

Throughout the history of philosophy, nobody has been able to bridge the gap between “is” and “ought.” Most efforts are really answers to other questions, such as “What is the nature of Man?” Lofty and philosophical as these speculations are, they are factual, not ethical. They may abstract from what “is,” but they do not obligate it. There is also a common, practical realm of casuistry that confronts a person's different moral judgments with each other. That is the ordinary function of ethics in our social world, as for instance in medical ethics. The limits to this are amusingly evident in psychological experiments that show people judging actions on varying principles (“I shouldn't push the person off the bus to save the others, but I shouldn't avoid driving into him if that would endanger the others.” “I may kill him but I shouldn't torture him”). In no case is the overarching rule itself subject to logical debate.

Freud taught that moral judgments are individually derived from our childhood (we abbreviate the process in the term superego (see Friedman 1956). Individual conscience gives us our marching orders. Ricoeur (1970, pp. 519-524) adds to this that we are pre-equipped with an open file for “oughts” and our psychodynamics simply fill in the particulars. That is probably the best bone we can throw to the philosophic moralist, though he might find additional nourishment in recent evidence that even some details, such as the rule of fairness, may be inborn. But the fact that most of us make moral judgments does not tell us that we should make moral judgments, and if someone is lacking that category, there is no way to demonstrate that he should have it. In particular, of course, desire is a matter of fact, and not a voluntary act. Analysts believe that desire is the rain that falleth from the heavens upon the earth below.

The general features of a “world” created by conscience are described by Kant (1781, 1788): Within our individual moral world, the rules dictated by conscience are universal and apply equally to all people, whether they agree with us or not. If they don't agree with us we judge them to be morally wrong.

Evocative Space: Where Listening Begins

Forrest Hamer

Forrest Hamer

5305 College Avenue
Oakland, CA 94618
E-mail: forrest.hamer@sbcglobal.net

Forrest Hamer   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

Genesis

There are endless poems

in the world,

and each one seeks its listeners out.

A poet hears first some hint,

coaxes this

into the body

where listening begins

between them—the poem

making the poet,

the poet making the poem.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Case for Analysis with the Baby in the Consulting Room

Jane Kite

Jane Kite

209 Pearl Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: jvkite@gmail.com

Jane Kite  

The point at which analyst, mother, and baby actually meet in the consulting room has a long prehistory. They are familiars. Patient and analyst have been in the room together with a baby in mind before conception, and with the baby alive and often visibly kicking from the inside for several months before it actually arrives in the room. The paradox of a baby who is there and not there, dreamed into life (in the Bionian sense) by analyst and patient while still just a representation in their minds, poses questions that are centrally important in how we conceive of analysis. I am thinking in particular of the role of unconscious communication between analyst and analysand as they jointly prefigure the baby and the astonishing experience of becoming a mother. The analytic dyad has become an analytic triad, and by the time the baby is actually brought into the consulting room as a separate person, he or she already exists as a shared object. The baby's arrival in the consulting room then signals a new phase in the analysis, and that is the transformation of shared maternal reverie into actual roles: mother, baby, and analyst.

As you may have picked up on by now, analysis during pregnancy and the postpartum period is, in my view, a privileged piece in an ongoing analysis; pregnancy and the introduction of the baby into the analysis as both a fantastic construction and a real object constitutes a singular experience for patient and analyst alike. Far from an awkward parameter, from this vantage point babies in the consulting room are a vital aspect of the patient's transition into “mother,” and the analyst's transition to a relationship with both the patient and her child, who, in addition to being a real baby, will always be an object in the analyst's mind in relation to the mother.

Training and Supervising Analyst, PINE Psychoanalytic Center.

The author acknowledges Ilse K. Jawetz for her countless unspoken contributions to this text over the last thirty-five years. Submitted for publication January 19, 2012.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Unconscious Reactions to the Baby in the Consulting Room

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: lynnezeavin@mindspring.com

Lynne Zeavin  

I begin with a personal example: I am standing in the kitchen with three good friends—one of whom has pictures of a new little grand-daughter, about four weeks old. My friends and I, all of us analysts, stand around, wide-eyed and giddy, looking at each photograph, taking our time, pointing out her features, the quality of her expression, her perfect mouth. Each of us is excited, moved, absorbed by the features of this brand-new baby girl. She is the first grandchild among this group of friends, and that is certainly a part of the response. But, more than that, I am quite certain that we are all taken back in time to memories of our own babies, and the primal feelings of longing elicited by the sight of this baby. The intensity of the reaction is both about this baby and about what babies can evoke in us more generally. The sight of a new baby stirs wishing, perhaps regret, perhaps a feeling of wanting, a feeling that never quite goes away. Babies are embodiments of time and continuity, the shifting, ongoing unfolding of relationships between parents and children, of time that is irretrievable yet vividly recalled via identification with the mother of the baby who is imagined though not seen. As we all look at the photographs there is an unmistakable mood both of possibility and of loss having to do with the recognition that our babies are grown—that the time of their babyhood is passed, and yet, the stirrings caused by a new baby make it seem as though it were all still possible again.

There is always a background when a baby appears in treatment.

Faculty, The Institute for Psychoanalytic Education affiliated with NYU Medical School; Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, NYU Langone Medical Center.

responseSet.title: Seeing and Being Seen: Emerging from a Psychic Retreat. By John Steiner. New York: Routledge, 2011, xviii + 196 pp., $38.99.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Seeing and Being Seen: Emerging from a Psychic Retreat. By John Steiner. New York: Routledge, 2011, xviii + 196 pp., $38.99.

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: lynnezeavin@mindspring.com

Lynne Zeavin  

John Steiner is perhaps best known in the U.S. for his first book, Psychic Retreats. “Psychic retreat” is the name Steiner gave to a system of

631

complex defenses used to master anxieties related to separateness, envy, and various persecutory fantasies regarding early objects. A combination of complex defensive operations gives rise to the creation of a pathological organization. Steiner has argued that the retreat functions as a psychic haven for narcissistically vulnerable patients. It affords a protective structure “where self and object are so fused, often with one inside the other, that there is insufficient separateness for either to be properly viewed” (p. 171). For Steiner such an organization defends against the very reality of object relations, because “reality involves separate objects, each with their own individual characteristics” (p. 171).

The process of exiting a psychic retreat can be perilous: The patient, fearful of being without protection, is not yet equipped with alternative ways of dealing with anxiety and mental pain. As subject and object achieve more separation within the personality, envy and/or humiliation may be pronounced. In this wonderful new book, Seeing and Being Seen, Steiner describes the many difficulties encountered by patients as they attempt to move out of a psychic retreat into greater contact with the analyst, psychic life, and the world more generally. The book's eight essays are presented in three parts, each elaborating an aspect of what occurs as patients emerge out of a pathological organization.

Part I includes an exploration of shame and humiliation, both typically downplayed in the Kleinian literature. Steiner's investigation of shame takes him into the realm of “seeing and being seen,” an area he explores with great sensitivity. Steiner's insight here is to realize how powerful the sense of exposure and attendant shame are as the patient leaves the retreat and the protection afforded by the pathological organization. Via numerous clinical examples, Steiner shows the variety of ways the affects associated with shame and humiliation can manifest in the analysis as the patient attempts to emerge from a retreat. Part II deals with helplessness, power, and dominance. With a focus on the analytic relationship, Steiner takes up both the paranoid and the depressive outcomes of the oedipus complex. Part III is organized around the crucial notions of mourning, melancholia, the repetition compulsion, and the death instinct.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Sisters in Women' Development. By Sue A. Kuba. New York: Oxford University Press, 2011, xx + 396 pp., $55.00.

Jeannie Vivona

Jeannie Vivona

11 Bala Avenue
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004
E-mail: jvivona@jcnj.edu

Jeannie Vivona  

In this ambitious undertaking, the psychologist Sue Kuba presents a detailed portrayal of her qualitative research study of women's experiences of their sister relationships and considers her findings in the context of the literature in four broad realms: characteristics of the sister relationship when one sister is disabled; experiences of lesbian women; gender roles and family relationships of women from a wide range of cultural and national backgrounds; and clinical theory from selected psychoanalytic, feminist, and family systems perspectives. Kuba's range of consideration is breathtaking, her efforts at integration across contents, methods, and philosophies herculean. The results, in the end, are mixed. The interview data are rich and thought-provoking, but her research approach lacks rigor and her analytic strategy is ill-suited to her aim of exploring the clinical implications of her findings. She identifies many aspects of the sister relationship that have not been adequately appreciated, but does not consider the existing literature on siblings, particularly the psychoanalytic literature, in sufficient depth. And though her efforts to integrate her findings with the literature are vigorous, they are limited in depth and scope by data that are both overly comprehensive and incomplete.

The book comprises three major sections. The first, “The Lifelong Relationship,” is devoted to a lengthy presentation (a good half of the book) of Kuba's qualitative research, for which she interviewed women about their relationships with their sisters. The opening chapter documents the absence of the sister relationship (and the sibling relationship more generally) from a range of psychoanalytic theories, as well as from relational cultural theory (especially the work of Jean Baker Miller); family therapy is also briefly considered. This absence of sisters is striking, as it spans theories developed by child analysts (e.g., Klein) and feminist thinkers (e.g., Miller). Kuba discovers that siblings appear in the clinical cases of many theorists, but not in their theorizing. She argues that the neglect of siblings and sisters in the clinical literature can be explained in part by the vagaries of each theorist's own sibling relationships. Similarly, others (e.g., Coles 2003) have argued that Freud's sibling-related ambivalence and rivalries impeded his full recognition of sibling dynamics.

[[RunningHead]]

Cultural Commentaries

Alfred Kazin's Journals. Edited by Richard M. Cook. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2011, xxiv + 598 pp., $45.00.

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

451 West End Avenue, Suite 1J
New York, NY 10024-5359
E-mail: sjcoenmd@aol.com

Stanley Coen   Training and Supervising Analyst and Senior Associate Director for Academic Affairs, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University.

After visiting the shtetl of Raciaz, Poland, sixty miles north of Warsaw, where my father and his family had lived until shortly after World War I—which I did in 2011—I found myself intrigued with issues of immigration, immigrants, and children of immigrants. I vividly recalled staying up all night reading Alfred Kazin's A Walker in the City (1951) during my first year in medical school—captivated by that memoir of a child of immigrants. For I too was one.

Kazin by then was well into his distinguished career. He began writing for The New Republic at age nineteen. At twenty-seven he published On Native Grounds (1942), which Lionel Trilling considered “quite the best and most complete treatment we have of an arduous and difficult subject” (quoted in Leader 2011). Kazin followed Edmund Wilson and Malcolm Cowley as literary editor of The New Republic, wrote for Fortune, and published frequently in The New York Times Book Review, The New York Review of Books, and The New Yorker. He lectured at prominent colleges and universities, published thirteen books, and became a literary star. But despite his meteoric rise from humble beginnings with an illiterate mother, Gita Fagelman, and a house painter father, Gedahlia Kazin (who he claimed was not very good at his craft), both Yiddish-speaking immigrants from Russia, he suffered persistent discontent. Neither Columbia nor Harvard would give him permanent teaching positions, and being a literary critic was not as satisfying as writing creative works like Saul Bellow, Philip Roth, Isaac Bashevis Singer, Norman Mailer, and Arthur Miller did, or his earlier heroes, Emerson, Thoreau, Melville, and Whitman.

Kazin's now published Journals (which he kept from 1933 to 1998)
